steps.title: Identify the horizontal asymptote
steps.content: Observe that as $x$ becomes increasingly negative (moving left), the curve approaches the $x$-axis. The function approaches $y = 0$. For $f(x) = ab^x + c$, the horizontal asymptote is $y = c$, so $c = 0$ and the function simplifies to $f(x) = ab^x$.

steps.title: Determine the sign of the leading coefficient
steps.content: The entire curve in the graph lies below the $x$-axis—all function values are negative. This tells us $a < 0$.

steps.title: Analyze when an exponential function equals zero
steps.content:
For $f(x) = ab^x$ to equal zero, we need $ab^x = 0$. However, for any positive base $b$ and any real exponent $x$, we have $b^x > 0$ always.

Since $a < 0$ (a negative constant), the product $ab^x$ is negative × positive, which is always negative.

An exponential function with a non-zero leading coefficient can never equal zero—it can only approach its asymptote.

The curve never touches or crosses the $x$-axis. That matches choice D — D is correct.
